findings.title: Unsupported batched operations clear the deferred solvency flag and enable undercollateralized borrows/withdrawals

best_agent_match.title: Unsupported operations clear the deferred solvency flag and enable uncollateralized borrows

findings.title: Oracle failures and zero exchange rates are accepted, allowing stale-price borrowing and zero-rate solvency bypasses

best_agent_match.title: Oracle failures fall back to stale or zero exchange rates, breaking solvency and liquidation

findings.title: Interest-rate changes are applied retroactively to already elapsed debt

best_agent_match.title: Interest-rate changes are applied retroactively to already elapsed debt

findings.title: Any clone deployed without atomic initialization can be permanently captured because `init()` is unrestricted

best_agent_match.title: Any uninitialized clone can be permanently captured because `init()` is unrestricted

rejected_candidates.title: Missing array length validation in liquidate function
rejected_candidates.reason: A shorter `maxBorrowParts` array only causes a bounds-check revert, and extra trailing entries are ignored; this does not liquidate the wrong user or create theft.

rejected_candidates.title: Unlimited users can be liquidated in single transaction causing DoS
rejected_candidates.reason: The caller chooses the array length and only risks its own transaction running out of gas; it does not block other liquidators from submitting smaller calls.

rejected_candidates.title: No slippage protection in liquidation
rejected_candidates.reason: The liquidator chooses whether to use a swapper and bears execution risk; if the required senUSD is not provided back, the final transfer reverts the whole transaction.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing reentrancy guards on critical functions
rejected_candidates.reason: No concrete reentrant exploit is supported by the cited code. The report is generic speculation about external calls.

rejected_candidates.title: Oracle rate not validated in liquidation
rejected_candidates.reason: Merged into F-002 as part of the broader stale/zero oracle-rate issue.

rejected_candidates.title: Anyone can manipulate exchange rate via performOperations
rejected_candidates.reason: `OPERATION_UPDATE_PRICE` only fetches the oracle's value; the caller cannot set an arbitrary exchange rate.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing validation for zero exchange rate
rejected_candidates.reason: Merged into F-002 as part of the broader zero/stale exchange-rate issue.

rejected_candidates.title: Unbounded iteration in performOperations
rejected_candidates.reason: This is caller-controlled gas consumption, not a protocol-level denial of service against other users.

rejected_candidates.title: Oracle price can be zero in OPERATION_UPDATE_PRICE
rejected_candidates.reason: Merged into F-002 as part of the broader zero/stale exchange-rate issue.

rejected_candidates.title: Liquidator can be any address without access control
rejected_candidates.reason: Permissionless liquidation is intentional protocol behavior, not a vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: No minimum borrow amount check
rejected_candidates.reason: Borrowing zero does not create realistic protocol harm beyond trivial dust state changes.

rejected_candidates.title: Swapper not validated for malicious behavior
rejected_candidates.reason: The liquidator supplies the swapper and the transaction reverts if repayment is not delivered; this is user-selected execution risk, not protocol theft.

rejected_candidates.title: accruedInterest.lastAccrued can be zero initially
rejected_candidates.reason: `init()` immediately calls `accumulate()`, which sets `lastAccrued` before any debt exists.

rejected_candidates.title: No access control on changeInterestRate decrease
rejected_candidates.reason: `changeInterestRate()` is already restricted to `onlyMasterContractOwner`; choosing a lower rate is governance policy, not missing access control.

rejected_candidates.title: Blacklist can be bypassed in direct function calls
rejected_candidates.reason: The blacklist is only used to restrict arbitrary external `OPERATION_CALL` targets; it is not intended as a global user access-control list.
